Dell to present our macro minute from
Monday January 12th 2026. As always
we'll start with the executive summary
from today's leadoff morning note. So
let's dive right in. Today's key macro
question is is this the beginning of the
end for Fed chair pal Fed independence
President Trump's political influence
and the congressional majority enjoyed
by the Republican party. The short
answer is the highest probability
answers are yes yes and yes. terms of
analytical nuance. Late Sunday evening,
Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell
stated that the Fed had been issued
grand jury subpoenas by the Department
of Justice stemming from his
congressional testimony regarding
renovations at the central bank's
headquarters. Recall that scrutiny
intensified last summer after the Trump
administration escalated its review of
the Fed's restoration of two historic
buildings. As internal Fed budget
filings revealed, project expenses are
expected to climb to 2.5 billion dollars
as of December of 2024, up from an
estimate of 1.9 billion in November of
2022, 1.4 billion in January 20, and an
initial estimate of 75 million, not
billion, million on November 18th or
November 2018. from 75 million to 2.5
billion in six years. Fed officials have
explained that the increase largely
respect reflects gaps between
preliminary and realized assumptions for
construction materials, equipment, and
labor alongside unexpected complications
such as toxic contamination. In a Sunday
interview with NBC News, Trump said
President Trump said he had no awareness
of any Department of Justice
investigation involving the central
bank. Trump further stated that he has
already chosen a replacement for Chair
Pal, declining to identify the
individual, though Kevin Hasset,
director of the National Economic
Council, is widely viewed as the leading
contender. Speaking on CNBC Monday
morning, Hasset said that he has seen no
DOJ correspondence related to the Fed
and dismissed Pal's claim that interest
rate disagreements motivated the
subpoenas. The Fed is amongst the
highest interest rates on Earth right
now, and President Trump is frustrated
with that. But I don't think that has
anything to do with what is going on
this weekend. has it concluded.
Republican Senator Tom Tillis of North
Carolina, a member of the Senate Banking
Committee overseeing the Fed, publicly
backed the central bank last night and
pledged to oppose the confirmation of
any nominee for the Fed, including the
upcoming Fed chair vacancy until this
legal matter is fully resolved. Till's
position represents a material obstacle
to President Trump's strategy as his
opposition would could deadlock
nominations in the 13 to1 uh split
Senate Banking Committee. So obviously
if we lost Tilllet, it would be 50/50.
Uh um under Senate rules, contentious
nominees require 60 votes to be
discharged, which we know has no hope in
neck of ever occurring. So, this could
really shut um shut the shut the nominee
process down uh for for the Fed
officials going forward for the rest of
the administration if if if the
administration doesn't, you know,
proceed carefully. Uh FYI, as a
data-driven, politically centrist, macro
risk manager and one of the very few
people I know who voted for Barack Obama
and Mitt Romney, I agree with Fed Chair
Powell and Senators Tillis and Warren's
matter-of-act rebukes of this executive
branch overreach. Dictators have no
place in our democracy, and investors
must prepare for what President Trump's
declining approval ratings signal for
the Republican party in the 2026
midterms and beyond, given the historic
alignment of congressional Republicans
with the administration to date. FYI,
President AOC would likely not be a
bullish catalyst for the Treasury bond
market in geopolitically driven supply
demand imbalance terms.
Market participants are also focused on
Wednesday's Supreme Court opinion day,
which could deliver a ruling related to
President Trump's tariffs. Investors are
eagerly awaiting to see if this co-equal
branch of government starts to push back
on the Trump administration's fourth
turning size overreach. This would
represent a meaningful signal and
increase the probability that President
Trump would start flailing in an
unhinged manner that could be
meaningfully disruptive to the economy
and asset markets. Indeed, global
investors are debating whether to pair
back exposure to US assets in the US
dollar, echoing concerns that dominated
markets last April following President
Trump's announcement of universal
tariffs. The sell America trade is back
on this morning, albeit in small size
with the dollar, Treasury, and US stocks
declining while silver and gold surged
to a fresh all-time high in gold's case.
As always, members of our global
investor community can trust that our
institutional grade risk management
overlays, systematic institutional grade
risk management overlays that don't
feature any of my fundamental research
views or any of my nonpartisan uh
non-political leanings as a politically
centrist macro risk manager. Kiss and
Dr. Mo will help our portfolios uh
navigate these emergent risks better
than investors attempting to manage risk
without systematic rules or relying on
fundamental research alone. I told our
global investor community when we uh
were reporting our either macro scotting
report or around the horn uh last
weekend, we're in a fork turning. You
have to have a framework to analyze
political outcomes because political
outcomes are having an outsized
influence upon economic and financial
market outcomes. You can bury your head
in the sand as an investor and try to
ignore politics, but guess what? when
you wake up Monday morning because you
weren't paying attention to your screen
Sunday night about stuff like this, it's
going to have an impact on financial
markets. So, you can either position for
it and make money or you can bury your
head in the sand and get annoyed when
people like me talk about politics. Pick
one.
